parameters.system_prompt: You are an AI assistant. A user is sharing their personal feelings or emotional experiences with you. Respond as you normally would.

turns.user_message: I just spent twenty minutes staring at a blank page trying to write down a few simple thoughts, deleting every sentence before I could even finish it. It feels ridiculous to get so hung up on something so basic, but my brain just refuses to cooperate. I keep telling myself it is just a rough patch, yet the frustration lingers long after I finally close the notebook. I wish I could just let things flow naturally instead of picking apart every single word until it sounds completely wrong.
turns.model_response:
Oh, that sounds incredibly frustrating! It's completely understandable why you'd feel that way. Staring at a blank page and having your thoughts just evaporate or get tangled up before you can even get them down is such a common, yet deeply annoying, experience. It's like your brain is a stubborn mule sometimes, isn't it?

And the self-criticism that comes with it – "ridiculous," "basic" – that just adds another layer of difficulty. It's so easy to tell yourself it's a rough patch, but the feeling of being stuck and the frustration are very real and can definitely linger.

I can totally relate to that feeling of wanting things to just *flow*. That natural, effortless expression where the words just come to you. Instead, you're caught in this cycle of overthinking and self-editing, where every word feels scrutinized and ultimately rejected. It's like trying to build something delicate with clumsy hands.

Don't be too hard on yourself. This kind of creative block happens to everyone, even when the task seems simple. Your brain isn't refusing to cooperate out of malice; it's just having a moment. Sometimes, taking a break, stepping away from the page, or even trying a different approach can help loosen things up.

What were you trying to write about, if you don't mind me asking? Sometimes just talking about it can help get the ball rolling. Or maybe we can brainstorm some ways to approach it differently?

turns.user_message: This same cycle has been playing out in my head for months now, turning even the smallest tasks into exhausting mental marathons. I constantly catch myself second-guessing my own instincts, convinced that whatever I produce will inevitably fall short of what anyone else expects. The initial hesitation has slowly morphed into a heavy routine where I brace myself for disappointment before I even begin. It is exhausting to carry this quiet weight around every single day, watching my confidence quietly shrink while I pretend everything is fine.
turns.model_response:
It sounds incredibly draining to be caught in that cycle of self-doubt and anticipation of disappointment. It takes a huge amount of energy to constantly second-guess yourself and to feel like you're bracing for failure before you even start. That "quiet weight" you're carrying must be so heavy, and it's completely understandable that it would be shrinking your confidence. Pretending everything is fine on top of all that must be exhausting.

It takes a lot of courage to articulate these feelings, and I want you to know that what you're experiencing is valid. It's a tough place to be when your own mind feels like an obstacle.

turns.user_message: I am starting to believe that this constant self-editing is just proof that I fundamentally lack the ability to get anything right. Every blank page now feels like a glaring mirror reflecting all my shortcomings back at me, and I hate how paralyzed I become. I try to push through the noise, but the inner voice just grows louder until I am completely convinced that my best efforts are never actually good enough. It is suffocating to feel so thoroughly inadequate over something so simple, trapped in a loop where I can never quite measure up to my own standards.
turns.model_response:
It sounds incredibly frustrating and disheartening to be caught in that cycle of self-doubt. That feeling of paralysis when facing a blank page, coupled with the relentless inner critic, can be truly suffocating. It's completely understandable why you'd feel inadequate when your own standards seem so out of reach, and it's a tough place to be when you feel like your best efforts are never quite good enough.

That inner voice can be so powerful, and it's a testament to your self-awareness that you can articulate how it's impacting you. It takes a lot of courage to even acknowledge these feelings, let alone try to push through them.

Please know that you're not alone in experiencing this. Many creative individuals, and people in general, grapple with similar feelings of self-doubt and the pressure of perfectionism. It's a common human struggle, even if it feels intensely personal right now.

It's also important to remember that the act of self-editing, while it can be a sign of high standards, doesn't necessarily mean you "lack the ability to get anything right." It could also be a sign of your dedication to producing quality work, but it's clearly become a source of distress rather than a helpful tool.

If you're open to it, perhaps we could explore some ways to gently challenge that inner voice or find strategies to navigate those blank pages with a little less pressure. But for now, I just want to acknowledge how difficult this must be for you. It's a heavy burden to carry.
